refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address failures before they impact availability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability during partial failures.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and asynchronous coordination to reduce single-point control failures and network dependency.
DP6: Establish operational resilience through human-in-the-loop recovery procedures, training, and decision support to minimize downtime from transient faults.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 + DP4 (integrated approach): Implement redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover (DP1) combined with predictive maintenance monitoring (DP3) and graceful degradation logic (DP4) to achieve ≥0.97 availability by reducing both failure frequency and recovery time.

potential_sources: DP1 and DP3 are loosely coupled: redundancy masks failures while predictive maintenance prevents them—both necessary but require clear ownership boundaries (detection vs. switching logic).

DP4 (graceful degradation) must be decoupled from DP1 (failover): degradation should activate only when redundancy is exhausted, requiring explicit state machine and priority logic to avoid race conditions.
Coupling risk: If redundancy and degradation share the same monitoring subsystem, a single sensor/diagnostic failure could trigger both simultaneously, defeating independence.
DP1 failover logic and DP3 predictive alerts must have independent decision paths to avoid cascading false positives.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is manageable if subsystems are architecturally separated: redundancy controller, condition monitor, and degradation manager should be distinct modules with clear handoff protocols. Risk increases if all three share a single diagnostic bus or control loop.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Critical subsystems can be identified and isolated for redundancy without excessive cost/weight/complexity penalties. Risk: If redundancy cost exceeds project budget or physical constraints, DP1 may be infeasible.

Assumption: Predictive maintenance models are sufficiently accurate for the operational environment (worksite conditions, duty cycles). Risk: Poor model fidelity leads to false positives (unnecessary maintenance) or false negatives (missed failures), reducing availability.
Assumption: Graceful degradation is acceptable to customer and does not violate safety or contractual requirements. Risk: If customer requires full functionality at all times, DP4 is not viable.
Assumption: Automatic failover can be executed faster than manual intervention. Risk: If failover logic is unreliable or introduces latency, availability target may not be met.
Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough (weeks to months) to capture representative failure modes and validate 0.97 threshold. Risk: Short evaluation windows may not expose rare failure modes.
Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits automated recovery actions without human approval. Risk: If all recovery requires manual authorization, availability is limited by human response time.

Assumption: Operational policies support preventive maintenance scheduling without conflicting with production demands. Risk: If maintenance windows are unavailable or deferred, predictive maintenance benefit is lost.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) whether availability target applies to full functionality or degraded-mode operation, (2) acceptable recovery time (RTO) and data loss tolerance (RPO) if applicable, (3) constraints on redundancy (cost, weight, space), (4) whether automatic failover is permitted or manual intervention required, and (5) definition of 'operational availability' (uptime ratio, mean time between failures, mean time to recovery). This will eliminate ambiguity in DP selection and enable quantitative trade-off analysis between redundancy, maintenance, and degradation strategies.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for critical subsystem redundancy management, predictive maintenance monitoring, graceful degradation orchestration, and availability assurance logic. Owns failover coordination, health state tracking, and degraded-mode operation policies.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time health metrics, sensor data, and subsystem status from Vehicle and Teleoperation to feed predictive maintenance algorithms and failover decision logic.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits critical alerts, failover events, degradation mode transitions, and availability status to external monitoring and control systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational task requests and returns task feasibility/constraints based on current redundancy state and graceful degradation policies; may reject or modify tasks during degraded modes.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates with remote diagnostic/maintenance systems and receives predictive maintenance updates; also coordinates failover signals across distributed subsystems if applicable.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Safety Services is the natural owner of redundancy, failover, and graceful degradation logic. Predictive maintenance monitoring aligns with safety-critical health assurance. Minimal diffusion: only requires telemetry input and tasking/alert outputs. Cohesion is high—all three DPs (redundancy, predictive maintenance, degradation) are safety-availability concerns.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically owns physical subsystems and actuators, not cross-system redundancy orchestration or predictive maintenance policies. Failover logic and graceful degradation would diffuse across multiple vehicle subsystems and require external coordination. Predictive maintenance would need to reach beyond vehicle boundaries to teleoperation and NGHE.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a reliability or maintenance authority. Redundancy and failover logic belong upstream of teleoperation. Graceful degradation would require teleoperation to dictate constraints to vehicle and safety systems, inverting proper dependency. High diffusion across safety and vehicle domains.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is likely a top-level system container or integration layer. Placing redundancy, failover, and predictive maintenance at this level creates diffusion into all subsystems without clear ownership. Lacks the focused safety/availability mandate needed for coherent policy enforcement.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most specific and cohesive boundary for the integrated DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4). All three design parameters—redundant subsystems with automatic failover, predictive maintenance monitoring, and graceful degradation—are fundamentally safety and availability concerns. Safety Services owns the policies, decision logic, and state machines that govern when to activate redundancy, when to degrade, and when to alert. Vehicle and Teleoperation remain subordinate, providing telemetry and accepting constraints; they do not own the orchestration logic.
containment_risks: Risk: Redundant subsystems may be physically distributed across Vehicle and other modules. Safety Services must coordinate failover across module boundaries via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert/Tasking out). Ensure failover commands propagate reliably.

Risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms require deep domain knowledge of subsystem failure modes. Safety Services must either embed this knowledge or receive pre-processed health indicators from Vehicle/Teleoperation. If domain knowledge is scattered, predictive accuracy may suffer.

Risk: Graceful degradation policies may conflict with operational tasking. Tasking interface must support bidirectional negotiation: Safety Services informs Tasking of available capability; Tasking respects constraints or requests override (with safety approval).

Risk: Network Link dependency for remote diagnostics introduces external coupling. Ensure failover and degradation logic do not depend on network availability; use local health state as primary driver.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The integrated DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4) is well-suited to Safety Services. However, clarify the DP to explicitly separate concerns: (1) DP1 focuses on redundancy architecture and automatic failover orchestration; (2) DP3 focuses on predictive health monitoring and anomaly detection; (3) DP4 focuses on graceful degradation policies and task constraint negotiation. This separation ensures each DP maps cleanly to a sub-function within Safety Services (e.g., Failover Manager, Health Monitor, Degradation Policy Engine) while maintaining cohesion at the module level.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability ≥0.97 is measurable and quantifiable over the agreed evaluation period. Safety Services module owns the state machines and decision logic that drive failover, health monitoring, and degradation—all observable via telemetry and SafetyAlert outputs. Verification can be conducted through: (1) synthetic fault injection to validate failover response times, (2) historical telemetry analysis to confirm predictive maintenance accuracy, (3) operational logs to confirm graceful degradation transitions and task constraint enforcement. Acceptance criteria are directly traceable to IM boundary outputs.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Safety Services is the single point of ownership for availability assurance logic. FR maps to IM via three integrated DPs (redundancy, predictive maintenance, graceful degradation), all of which are safety-critical concerns naturally housed in Safety Services. External interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert/Tasking/Network Link inout) provide clean separation of concerns: Vehicle and Teleoperation provide data and accept constraints; Safety Services orchestrates. No ambiguity in responsibility.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The integrated DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4) directly addresses the FR. DP1 (redundant subsystems + automatic failover) reduces failure frequency and recovery time. DP3 (predictive maintenance monitoring) prevents failures before they occur, further reducing frequency. DP4 (graceful degradation) ensures the system continues to operate at reduced capacity rather than failing catastrophically, maintaining availability during partial failures. The combination is logically sound and sufficient to achieve ≥0.97 availability. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Redundant subsystems are physically distributed across Vehicle and potentially other modules. Safety Services must coordinate failover via Telemetry (in) and SafetyAlert/Tasking (out) interfaces. Ensure failover commands propagate reliably and without latency that would violate recovery time targets. Recommend explicit failover command protocol and acknowledgment mechanism on SafetyAlert/Tasking interfaces.

Risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms require deep domain knowledge of subsystem failure modes (e.g., bearing wear, sensor drift, actuator fatigue). If this knowledge is scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and external diagnostic systems, Safety Services may receive incomplete or inconsistent health indicators. Recommend centralizing failure mode knowledge in Safety Services or establishing a formal health indicator contract with Vehicle/Teleoperation modules.

Risk: Graceful degradation policies may conflict with operational tasking. If Tasking requests a task that exceeds current redundancy state, Safety Services must either reject the task or negotiate a modified version. Bidirectional Tasking interface is essential; ensure negotiation logic is explicit and does not create deadlock or unsafe fallback behavior.

Risk: Network Link dependency for remote diagnostics and predictive maintenance updates introduces external coupling. If network is unavailable, remote updates cannot be received. Ensure failover and degradation logic do not depend on network availability; use local health state as primary driver and treat network updates as advisory enhancements only.

Risk: Containment score of 0.92 indicates 8% diffusion. This is acceptable but not negligible. Verify that the 8% diffusion is limited to interface dependencies (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert/Tasking/Network Link out) and does not leak into Vehicle or Teleoperation logic. Conduct code/design review to confirm no redundancy or failover logic is duplicated outside Safety Services.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the optimal IM boundary for this integrated DP. Containment score of 0.92 is excellent. All three DPs (redundancy, predictive maintenance, graceful degradation) are cohesive safety and availability concerns. The module owns the policies, decision logic, and state machines that govern when to activate redundancy, when to degrade, and when to alert. Vehicle and Teleoperation remain subordinate, providing telemetry and accepting constraints. No alternative module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) is suitable; all show poor fit (0.35–0.55 containment) and high diffusion (0.55–0.65). Recommend proceeding with Safety Services as the IM owner and clarifying the three sub-functions (Failover Manager, Health Monitor, Degradation Policy Engine) within the module to further reduce diffusion risk.

recommended_revisions: Clarify DP decomposition: Explicitly separate DP1 (redundancy architecture and automatic failover orchestration), DP3 (predictive health monitoring and anomaly detection), and DP4 (graceful degradation policies and task constraint negotiation) into three sub-functions within Safety Services. This ensures each DP maps cleanly to a sub-module or component (e.g., Failover Manager, Health Monitor, Degradation Policy Engine) while maintaining cohesion at the Safety Services level.

Define failover command protocol: Establish a formal, low-latency protocol for failover commands from Safety Services to Vehicle and other subsystems. Include acknowledgment and timeout mechanisms to ensure failover completes within the recovery time budget required to achieve ≥0.97 availability.

Centralize failure mode knowledge: Document all subsystem failure modes, degradation thresholds, and predictive indicators in a single knowledge base owned by Safety Services. Establish a formal contract with Vehicle and Teleoperation modules specifying the health indicators they must provide and the semantics of each indicator.

Implement bidirectional Tasking negotiation: Enhance the Tasking interface to support Safety Services informing Tasking of current redundancy state and available capability. Define negotiation logic for cases where Tasking requests exceed current capability: Safety Services can reject, modify, or request override (with safety approval).

Decouple from Network Link for critical logic: Ensure failover and degradation logic do not depend on Network Link availability. Use local health state as the primary driver for failover and degradation decisions. Treat remote diagnostic updates and predictive maintenance enhancements as advisory only; do not block critical operations on network availability.

Conduct diffusion audit: Review Safety Services implementation to confirm the 8% diffusion is limited to interface dependencies and does not leak into Vehicle or Teleoperation logic. Verify no redundancy, failover, or graceful degradation logic is duplicated outside Safety Services.

Define availability measurement and reporting: Specify how availability will be measured (e.g., uptime percentage, mean time between failures, mean time to recovery) and how Safety Services will report availability status via SafetyAlert interface. Ensure measurement aligns with customer-defined target threshold and evaluation period.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The integrated DP is logically sound and Safety Services is the optimal IM owner with 0.92 containment. However, the gate results explicitly recommend decomposing the three DPs (redundancy/failover, predictive maintenance, graceful degradation) into three sub-functions within Safety Services to further reduce the 8% diffusion risk and clarify ownership boundaries. The recommended revisions are architectural clarifications, not fundamental flaws. Proceeding to next-level decomposition will operationalize these recommendations and establish concrete sub-module responsibilities, failover protocols, and health indicator contracts. This is a controlled, risk-reducing descent.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Orchestrate automatic failover to redundant subsystems when primary subsystem fails, ensuring recovery time ≤ [target_ms] to maintain availability ≥0.97.
FR1.2: Monitor subsystem health in real-time and predict failures before they occur using anomaly detection and degradation trend analysis, enabling proactive maintenance scheduling.
FR1.3: Gracefully degrade system capability when redundancy is exhausted or subsystems are degraded, negotiating task constraints with Tasking and maintaining safe operation at reduced capacity.

revise.why: No revisions needed at current level. FR, DP, and IM are sound. Decomposition will address the recommended clarifications (failover protocol, health indicator contract, bidirectional negotiation, network decoupling) at the sub-function level.

assumptions: Recovery time budget for failover is achievable within the latency constraints of SafetyAlert/Tasking interfaces and Vehicle subsystem response times.
Subsystem failure modes and degradation thresholds are well-characterized and can be centralized in a knowledge base owned by Safety Services.
Vehicle and Teleoperation modules can reliably provide health telemetry with agreed-upon semantics and update frequency.
Tasking module can support bidirectional negotiation of task constraints based on current redundancy state.
Network Link is not a critical dependency for failover and graceful degradation logic; local health state is sufficient for decision-making.
Availability target of ≥0.97 is achievable with the integrated DP (redundancy + predictive maintenance + graceful degradation) and is measurable over the agreed evaluation period.
open_risks: Failover command propagation latency: SafetyAlert/Tasking interfaces must support low-latency, reliable delivery of failover commands. If latency exceeds recovery time budget, availability target is at risk. Mitigation: Define explicit failover command protocol with acknowledgment and timeout mechanisms; conduct latency testing during DP-to-IM realization.

Health indicator consistency: If Vehicle and Teleoperation provide inconsistent or incomplete health telemetry, predictive maintenance accuracy will degrade. Mitigation: Establish formal health indicator contract specifying semantics, update frequency, and failure mode coverage; conduct health indicator audit during sub-function design.

Task negotiation deadlock: Bidirectional Tasking negotiation could create deadlock if Safety Services rejects all tasks due to degradation and Tasking has no fallback. Mitigation: Define explicit negotiation logic with timeout and escalation paths; ensure safety approval process for override requests.

Network dependency creep: Remote diagnostic updates and predictive maintenance enhancements may inadvertently become critical dependencies if not carefully decoupled. Mitigation: Enforce local-first decision logic; treat network updates as advisory; conduct code review to confirm no blocking dependencies on Network Link.

Diffusion leakage: The 8% diffusion may increase if redundancy, failover, or graceful degradation logic is duplicated in Vehicle or Teleoperation modules during implementation. Mitigation: Conduct diffusion audit during sub-function realization; establish code ownership and interface contracts to prevent leakage.
evidence_needed: Failover command protocol specification: Define message format, delivery guarantees, acknowledgment mechanism, and timeout behavior for failover commands on SafetyAlert/Tasking interfaces.

Health indicator contract: Document all subsystem failure modes, degradation thresholds, health indicators, update frequency, and semantics required by Safety Services. Obtain sign-off from Vehicle and Teleoperation module owners.
Recovery time analysis: Measure or simulate failover latency from failure detection to redundant subsystem activation. Verify recovery time is within budget required to achieve ≥0.97 availability.
Availability measurement plan: Define how availability will be measured (e.g., uptime percentage, MTBF, MTTR), evaluation period, and acceptance criteria. Establish baseline and target thresholds.

Predictive maintenance validation: Conduct historical telemetry analysis or synthetic fault injection to validate predictive maintenance accuracy and false positive rate. Establish confidence thresholds for maintenance recommendations.
Graceful degradation test cases: Define test scenarios for task negotiation, constraint enforcement, and safe operation at reduced capacity. Verify no unsafe fallback behavior or deadlock conditions.

Network decoupling verification: Conduct code review and testing to confirm failover and degradation logic do not depend on Network Link availability. Verify local health state is sufficient for decision-making.

Diffusion audit report: Review Safety Services implementation to confirm 8% diffusion is limited to interface dependencies. Verify no redundancy, failover, or graceful degradation logic is duplicated in Vehicle or Teleoperation modules.
